segments.s530-g25: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. آسوده گردید is a past action: آسوده (at ease) + گردید (became) — the predicate 'became at ease'. یکسر is an adverb modifying the following directional phrase 'به بزم' and means 'entirely/wholly' (or 'at once'); word order here is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: subject implied (they) + predicate + adverb + prepositional goal 'to the feast'.
segments.s530-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason or consequence ('for/that'). زود آید is narrative present ('soon comes' — آید is present simple used in storytelling). اندیشه is the grammatical subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion): 'the thought comes soon'. روز رزم is a genitive phrase 'the day of battle' (ezāfe omitted in verse).
segments.s530-g27: دلیران is the plural subject 'the brave'. به خوردن + نهادند + سر is an idiomatic sequence: سر نهادن به خوردن (here word order is altered) — نهادند is the past tense verb 'they put/placed' (from نهادن), so the phrase means 'they put their heads to eating' i.e. 'they began to eat'.
segments.s530-g28: چو آسوده شد is 'when (it/they) became at ease' (چو = when, شد = became). کردگاه و کمر are the coordinated nouns serving as the patient/experiencer of آسوده شدن; کردگاه = 'site/scene, the place of action (hall/court)' and کمر here means 'girdle/belt' (the war-belt, figuratively the arms), so the clause says these places/vestments were at ease. Verb شد is singular but governs the compound subject in poetic usage.
segments.s530-g29: پراندیشه رزم شد is a predicative cluster: پراندیشه ('full of thought', an adjectival/participial form) modified by رزم ('of battle' — genitive), followed by شد 'became'. The subject اردشیر follows the verb (inverted order common in poetry): 'Ardashir became full of thoughts of war.'
segments.s530-g30: چو این داستان بشنوی is a conditional/temporal clause: چو = 'when/if', این داستان = 'this story', بشنوی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive ('you hear' / 'you should hear'). یادگیر is an exhortation 'learn / commit to memory' (imperative/hortative sense in context). The sequence addresses the listener: 'When you hear this tale, learn it.'
segments.s530-g9: برآهخت is a past, compound/perfect-like verbal form meaning "raised up" (bar-āhākht); it governs the following noun شمشیر as its direct object. The conjunction و links this action to a second past verb اندرنهاد (an older/poetic past of نهادن), which here lacks its object in this segment and thus anticipates the object that follows in the next segment.
segments.s530-g10: گیا را is the definite/marked object (را marks the specific thing acted on). ز خون is a prepositional phrase meaning "with/in blood" (ز here = from/with, poetic). بر سر افسر is a locative phrase "upon the top/head of the helmet" (سر = top/head of the helmet). The clause-final نهاد is the main past verb "placed/put" that takes گیا را as its object.
segments.s530-g11: همه دشت is the subject "the whole plain/entire plain." زیشان (literally "from/of them/among them") links the scene to the defeated people—it indicates that the following nouns belong to them. سر و دست شد is a predicative phrase with شد (past "became/was") and the coordinated nouns سر و دست describing what the plain "became"—i.e., covered with severed heads and hands.
segments.s530-g12: ز انبوه کشته is an ablative/causal phrase "from/because of the multitude of the slain" (ز = from/through). زمین is the subject "the ground/earth," and گست شد is a verbal phrase meaning "was spread/become an expanse" (گست here = expanse/extent; شد = became), describing how the earth was in consequence of the many dead.
segments.s530-g13: بی‌اندازه is an adverb meaning "excessively/beyond measure." زیشان again marks the affected group "of them/among them." گرفتار شد is a compound of the adjective/participial گرفتـار with شد (past "became") and functions as a predicative construction meaning "became beset/overwhelmed/ensnared."
segments.s530-g14: سترگی و نابخردی are two coordinated nouns ("greatness/majesty and folly"). خوار شد is a predicative phrase where خوار ("despised/humbled") is the adjective and شد is the past auxiliary/copula, so the clause means "greatness and folly became humbled/despised."
segments.s530-g15: همه بومهاشان is the direct object phrase "all their homelands/lands" (بومها = homelands; -شان = their). به تاراج داد is a light-verbal phrase where به تاراج ("to pillage/over to plunder") indicates the fate imposed on those lands and داد is the past tense "gave/allowed/handed over." The construction means their lands were given up to pillage.
segments.s530-g16: سپه را is marked with را, here marking the recipient/indirect object "to the army." همه بدره و تاج is the list of things given: همه "all/every," بدره (poetic) "beauties/ornaments" and تاج "crown/honor." داد is the past verb "gave," so the clause reads "he gave the army all ornaments and the crown."
segments.s530-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time clause. خورشید شد is a past/becoming construction ('the sun became/turned'), where شد is the past of شدن. زرد modifies خورشید (predicate adjective). لشکر براند places the agent/action after the temporal clause: لشکر (army) + براند (drove/put in motion); براند is an episodic past/preterite meaning 'drove/caused to move.'
segments.s530-g2: کسی را begins a post-positive phrase with the object/definite marker را; here کسی is used in the indefinite/negative-existential sense 'no one.' که introduces a relative clause describing that person. نابردنی is an adjective 'not-to-be-conquered / unconquerable.' بد بماند uses بد as an adverb/adjective 'badly/ill' with بماند (subjunctive/present of ماندن) 'remain/be left' — together giving 'remained in a bad state' or, in context, 'there remained no one who remained unconquerable.'
segments.s530-g3: چو introduces another temporal clause 'when.' شب نیم بگذشت is a time phrase 'half the night passed' where بگذشت is the past of گذشتن 'to pass' referring to elapsed time. و links the two predicates; تاریک شد is a simple past/resultative 'it became dark' (شد = became).
segments.s530-g4: جهاندار is the subject 'the world-ruler/sovereign.' با is the preposition 'with.' کرد here is ambiguous in the manuscript: it can be a verb 'did/made' but, in many classical lines, the same form also functions as a proper name/ethnonym (Kurd) or a substantive; none of the provided verbal senses fit that nominal reading, so it is marked below with -1. نزدیک شد is a compound verb phrase 'drew near/approached' (نزدیک = near/approaching; شد = became/approached as past).
segments.s530-g5: همه دشت sets up 'the whole plain' (همه = all/whole; دشت = plain). زیشان is a genitival/relational clitic meaning 'of them / from them' modifying پر از خفته. پر از is the collocation 'full of' (پر + از), followed by خفته 'sleepers/asleep'. دید is the past tense 'he saw' closing the clause.
segments.s530-g6: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'each and every.' دل لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the hearts of the army' (دل = heart; لشکر = army). آشفته is a predicate adjective 'disturbed/put into disorder.' دید is again the past 'he saw.' Word order is Subject omitted — verb-final perception verb sees the preceding NP as its object.
segments.s530-g7: چو آمد marks a temporal clause 'when he came.' سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). به بالین is a locative phrase 'to the bedside/at his side' (به = to/at; بالین here is 'beside/at the pillow'). کرد is the verb 'did/acted' (کرد here functions as an auxiliary/action verb: 'performed the act').
segments.s530-g8: This clause is a transitive clause whose verb is سپرد 'entrusted/handed over.' عنان 'reins' is the first direct object; باره is a deverbal/nominal element 'bearing / burden / load' (here used in a compound sense 'reins-bearing' or simply 'reins and burden'); تیزتگ is a noun (here read as 'the sharp blade' or possibly a name) and را is the definite object marker. سپرد is the past 'entrusted/handed over.' The sequence lists the things handed over before the verb, a common poetic word order.
segments.s530-g17: چنان شد که is a fixed narrative formula meaning "it became so / it happened that," with شد as the past-tense verb 'became/happened' introducing a result clause. دینار is the noun (the coin) and the following prepositional phrases بر سر (on the head) and به تشت (into/on the basin) give the location; the copula/verb is provided earlier by شد and is omitted for economy as is common in poetry.
segments.s530-g18: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پیر مردی is an indefinite phrase 'an old man' (پیر = old; مردی = a man / one man). ببردی is a 2nd-person past/subjunctive-form used for the protasis here and should be read as 'you took / you would take' (classical Persian uses such forms in conditional clauses). به دشت is the prepositional phrase 'to/into the plain.'
segments.s530-g19: به دینار او marks the theme/topic 'with regard to his dinar' (به + noun + possessive او). کس (anyone) + نکردی (negative past 'did not do') + نگاه (a noun 'a look/glance') is an idiomatic construction meaning 'no one cast a glance' or 'no one looked (at it)'; note that the verb نکردی is the past negative used here in a generalizing narrative sense.
segments.s530-g20: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / by reason of'. نیک‌اختر و بخت is a binomial 'good‑star (auspicious star) and fortune'—both abstract sources of good fate. وز داد شاه should be read as verb phrase with داد 'gave' and شاه 'the king' as agent: 'the king gave (it)'; وز in this context is an archaic/poetic particle/linker affecting nuance (see note).
segments.s530-g21: ز introduces the reason/ground 'from/because of'. مردی here is the abstract noun 'manliness' or 'heroic conduct' rather than simply 'a man'. نکردی is the past negative 'you did not show/do'. The following broken-up piece ب + دان + جنگ + فخر should be read together as بدانِ جنگِ فخر 'in/for that battle's pride' (ب as locative/prefix + دان functioning here as a demonstrative/poetic element 'that', followed by جنگ 'battle' and فخر 'pride/glory'). Overall: 'Because of manliness you did not do/you had no glory in that battle.'
segments.s530-g22: گرازان is a plural noun 'boars' (here collective—'a troop of boars' or simply 'the boars'). بیامد is the past simple 'came'. به شهر is the prepositional phrase 'into/unto the city', and صطخر is the place-name Istakhr. The clause states the simple action 'boars came to Istakhr.'
segments.s530-g23: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past of گفتن here meaning 'he commanded/ordered.' کاسپان is a vocative or direct object 'the Caspians' (people from the Caspian region). به نیرو کنید is an imperative phrase: به (toward/for) + نیرو (troops/force) + کنید (2nd‑pl. imperative of کردن 'do/make') — 'make/bring to strength; assemble into a force.'
segments.s530-g24: سلیح is an adjective/noun meaning 'armed' or 'weapon' (here 'arm' as a directive to equip) modifying سواران 'horsemen/riders'. بی‌آهو is a poetic expression: literally formed from بی‑ + آهو but used idiomatically here to mean 'like a gazelle' or 'fleet as a deer' (i.e., swift/nimble). کن کنید is the imperative 'make/do' (کنید = 2nd‑pl. imperative), so the whole clause means 'equip the horsemen and make them fleet (as gazelles).'

sentences.s530-s13: When they became at ease, they entirely went to the feast; for soon the thought of the day of battle came.
sentences.s530-s14: The brave laid their heads to eating (began to eat); when the hall and the girdle were at ease.
sentences.s530-s15: Ardashir became full of thoughts of battle; when you hear this story, take it to heart (learn/remember).
sentences.s530-s5: He raised his sword and then plunged it in, and set a sprig (giyā), stained with blood, upon the helmet.
sentences.s530-s6: The entire plain was strewn with their heads and hands.
sentences.s530-s7: They were seized with great affliction; their pride and folly were humbled.
sentences.s530-s8: He gave all their homelands to pillage, and to the army he gave every ornament and crown.
sentences.s530-s1: When the sun turned yellow he set the army in motion; there remained no one who could not be overcome.
sentences.s530-s2: When half the night had passed and it grew dark, the sovereign approached with (his) Kord.
sentences.s530-s3: He saw the whole plain full of their sleepers; he saw, one by one, the hearts of the army disturbed.
sentences.s530-s4: When the spahbed came to the bedside, he acted and handed over the reins and the sharp blade.
sentences.s530-s9: It came to pass thus: a dinar (coin) upon the head, into a basin — if you should take an old man out onto the plain.
sentences.s530-s10: No one cast a glance at his dinar; from good‑star and fortune the king bestowed (it).
sentences.s530-s11: You showed no manliness in that battle's boast; boars came into the city of Istakhr.
sentences.s530-s12: He commanded: 'Caspians, assemble into a force; arm the horsemen and make them fleet as gazelles.'
